questions.q: What does the CAP theorem state about distributed systems?
options: A system can guarantee at most two of three properties: Consistency, Availability, or Partition tolerance
All distributed systems must sacrifice Consistency to achieve Availability
Partition tolerance is optional and can be disabled in local area networks
Systems that prioritize Consistency will never experience network failures
questions.explanation: The CAP theorem proves that systems can achieve only two of the three guarantees in the presence of network partitions. Option 1 is an overgeneralization; some systems do sacrifice availability. Option 2 is false; partitions are fundamental to distributed systems.

questions.q: In CAP terminology, what does 'Consistency' mean?
options: All nodes see the same data at the same time (strong consistency)
Data is formatted uniformly across all nodes
The system persists all acknowledged writes to disk
Nodes gradually agree on data values without synchronous coordination
questions.explanation: CAP's consistency refers to strong consistency where all clients see the same data simultaneously. Option 1 is about data formatting. Option 2 is durability, not consistency in the CAP sense. Option 3 describes eventual consistency.

questions.q: What does 'Availability' mean in the CAP theorem?
options: Every request to any non-failing node receives a response without guarantee of consistency
The system never goes offline for maintenance or updates
All replicas have identical hardware capacity
The system responds to requests faster than the network latency
questions.explanation: Availability means the system always responds, though responses may be stale. Option 1 assumes zero downtime. Option 2 concerns hardware, not availability. Option 3 is about timing, not responsiveness.

questions.q: What is a network 'partition' in distributed systems?
options: A failure where nodes cannot communicate with each other, splitting the network into isolated groups
A division of data across multiple nodes for scalability
A temporary delay in network messages between nodes
A firewall that blocks certain types of network traffic
questions.explanation: A partition is a communication failure that isolates groups of nodes from each other. Option 1 describes data sharding, not partitions. Option 2 is a temporary delay, not a partition. Option 3 describes access control, not network failures.

questions.q: Why must a system choose between Consistency and Availability during a partition?
options: If nodes cannot communicate, maintaining consistency requires refusing some requests (sacrificing availability), or serving stale data (sacrificing consistency)
Partitions are rare and systems can simply wait for the network to heal
Consistency and Availability are properties that degrade equally with partitions
The CAP theorem only applies to systems with an odd number of nodes
questions.explanation: During a partition, a system must choose: either block requests to preserve consistency or risk inconsistency to maintain availability. Option 1 is false; partitions are inevitable. Option 2 assumes equal degradation, which is incorrect.

options: If Partition tolerance exists, systems trade Consistency for Latency or Availability; else, systems trade Consistency for Latency
PACELC is an acronym for five properties: Partition, Availability, Consistency, Eventual, Latency
PACELC disproves the CAP theorem by showing all three properties can coexist
PACELC applies only to systems where Partition tolerance is negligible
questions.explanation: PACELC extends CAP by stating that even without partitions, systems must trade Consistency for Latency. Option 1 falsely claims five properties suffice. Option 2 contradicts CAP. Option 3 is inapplicable universally.

questions.q: Which of the following is a CA system (Consistency + Availability, no partition tolerance)?
options: A traditional SQL database on a single reliable network with no replication across WAN
DynamoDB with eventual consistency enabled
A gossip protocol that replicates data across multiple data centers
A distributed blockchain system like Bitcoin
questions.explanation: CA systems exist only in reliable networks without partitions; single-server databases are practical CA systems. Option 1 provides eventual consistency. Option 2 replaces replication across regions, enabling partitions. Option 3 is a distributed system inherently subject to partitions.

questions.q: Why is Partition tolerance considered a requirement in modern distributed systems?
options: Network partitions are inevitable in large-scale systems, so systems must be designed to handle them
Partition tolerance improves system performance by reducing the need for consensus
Without partition tolerance, the CAP theorem is inapplicable
Partition tolerance allows systems to ignore Byzantine faults entirely
questions.explanation: Modern distributed systems span networks where partitions are inevitable, making partition tolerance non-negotiable. Option 1 is false; partitions actually decrease performance. Option 2 assumes partition tolerance is optional. Option 3 assumes Byzantine faults are covered by partition tolerance.

questions.q: In a CP system (Consistency + Partition tolerance), what happens during a network partition?
options: The system refuses some requests to maintain strong consistency across partitions
The system continues serving all requests with eventually consistent data
The system prioritizes latency over consistency guarantees
The system merges partitioned data using application-specific logic
questions.explanation: CP systems sacrifice availability to maintain consistency; they may block or reject requests during partitions. Option 1 provides eventual consistency, not strong consistency. Option 2 prioritizes latency, not consistency. Option 3 assumes merging is always possible.

questions.q: Why do many NoSQL databases choose AP over CP?
options: Applications often tolerate eventual inconsistency better than unavailability, and AP enables high availability and fault tolerance
AP systems are fundamentally faster than CP systems at handling writes
AP systems eliminate the need for data replication across regions
The CAP theorem guarantees AP systems will never lose committed data
questions.explanation: AP systems remain available during partitions with eventual consistency, which many applications can tolerate. Option 1 conflates speed with availability. Option 2 doesn't eliminate replication needs. Option 3 is false; data loss is possible in AP systems.
